critique.summary: The article reports that Honda is discontinuing its electric vehicle (EV) programs, including halting development of new EV models and stopping production of a GM-built EV. The author argues that this decision will hinder Honda's ability to compete in the evolving automotive industry, particularly in electric drivetrains and software-defined vehicles.
mainPoints: Honda has stopped development of its electric Acura RDX, Honda 0 sedan, and SUV models.
Honda will cease production of the Prologue, an EV designed and built by GM.
The company blames U.S. tariffs and Chinese competition but lacked a strong EV strategy from the start.
Honda risks falling behind in two major industry shifts: electric drivetrains and software-defined vehicles (SDVs).
Legacy automakers often struggle with EVs due to adapting existing internal combustion platforms rather than designing EVs from the ground up.
Honda is missing out on learning opportunities in EV development, manufacturing, supply chains, and customer feedback.
Software-defined vehicles, which allow over-the-air updates and advanced features, are becoming standard in EVs, but Honda has made little progress in this area.
Honda faces an identity crisis as a traditional internal combustion engine company in a market shifting towards EVs and autonomous technologies.
Honda's competitiveness in China has declined, contributing to significant financial losses.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about Honda discontinuing specific EV projects and the Prologue production are supported by reports from credible sources like Automotive News. The challenges faced by legacy automakers in transitioning to EVs and the importance of software-defined vehicles are well-documented trends in the automotive industry. The article accurately reflects Honda's recent financial struggles and competitive challenges in China, as reported in their earnings statements. However, some opinions about Honda's motivations and future prospects are speculative and framed from the author's critical perspective. Overall, the factual claims are largely accurate but the analysis includes subjective interpretation.
considerations: The article does not provide direct statements or responses from Honda addressing the discontinuation of EV programs beyond citing tariffs and competition.
It does not explore Honda's broader strategic plans, such as potential investments in hybrid technology or alternative mobility solutions.
The impact of global supply chain issues and semiconductor shortages on Honda's EV strategy is not discussed.
The article focuses primarily on the U.S. and Chinese markets without considering Honda's position in other regions.
Potential future changes in government policy or incentives that could affect Honda's EV plans are not mentioned.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Honda's decision to halt its EV development, with commenters debating the implications for Honda and the broader automotive industry. Many express concern about Honda falling behind in the EV and software-defined vehicle markets, while others highlight challenges like infrastructure, consumer preferences, and the evolving competitive landscape.

comments.author: TrackerFF
comments.text:
I live in a top EV market, Norway.

ICE cars have been planned out for years now, and something like 96% of all new cars in Norway were EV last year.

Basically, if you plan on keeping selling ICE cars, you're removing yourself from the market here. There's no future for new personal ICE cars here.

I figure most other countries will be the same.
comments.analysis: This comment provides accurate data about Norway's EV market dominance, illustrating the rapid shift away from ICE vehicles in leading regions.

comments.author: Denatonium
comments.text: Calling the Prologue "Honda's EV" feels like a huge stretch. The Prologue was a rebadged GM vehicle that served strictly as a compliance car for meeting CAFE standards. Now that the CAFE standards have been rendered toothless, there's no longer a need for that deal.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly identifies the Prologue as a rebadged GM vehicle, providing context on Honda's limited genuine EV offerings.

comments.author: rkagerer
comments.text:
Honda is setting itself up for failure on the second disruption sweeping the automotive industry: the software-defined vehicle (SDV), which has core capabilities that can be upgraded and improved over time.

No thank you. Not sure why the author frames this as a good thing. They've been bamboozled by the automakers and have got it backwards - you're buying a vehicle that already has the capabilities, but are disabled, then paying rent (or a fee) to turn them on. I'm much more likely to buy from a manufacturer that doesn't play these games.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully critiques Honda's strategy regarding software-defined vehicles, highlighting a key industry trend Honda risks missing.

comments.author: alliao
comments.text: To be honest, I have every faith in Honda. It took them a long time to arrive at hybrid, but they were never about first to market, but they were always adamant about controlling the entire technology stack.. made their own transmission and everything. And engineering doesn't faze them, Honda just nonchalantly displayed a reusable rocket like it was too easy... EV is a little bit like AI nowadays, not much moat and possibly not challenging enough for Honda R&D so why not. I'll always be on the look out for Honda's next take on EV.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a balanced perspective on Honda's historical approach to innovation and suggests potential for future success despite delays.

comments.author: small_model
comments.text: All the legacy automakers that haven't fully moved to EV's PROFITABLY will go defacto bankrupt within a few years, there will be some mergers to stay alive but it's game over. Tesla and China companies will own auto, with Tesla capturing most the profit, similar to Apple vs Android phones. Autonomy will further accelerate this.
comments.analysis: While somewhat speculative, the comment reflects a widely held industry view about the financial risks legacy automakers face if they lag in EV adoption.

comments.author: nonford150
comments.text:
I almost pulled the trigger on a Prologue; so glad I had second thoughts. Even though it was essentially a GM product, I've only ever owned Hondas, so I thought "Well, at least I can get service at my Honda dealer".

Charging in the US (other than at home) is still the biggest issue for me. I do lots of traveling, and waiting 30-45 minutes to charge even at a Level 3 charger is a PITA. If I had a J std charger, then it's even longer. This makes my monthly 8 hour trips one-way another 2 hours - this sucks. Sorry - I'll keep my 2005 Honda Element with 445K miles. Another engine would be cheaper than less than a year of car payments. And it's pretty much indestructible.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights a practical and well-known limitation of current EV infrastructure, especially relevant to long-distance travel.

comments.author: kleiba
comments.text:
> Consumers, mostly those who buy EVs from the likes of Tesla, Rivian, and BYD, have grown accustomed to the frequent updates, slick infotainment software, and advanced driver-assistance systems.

Guess which three items out of that list I do not want.
comments.analysis: The comment expresses skepticism about software-heavy EV features, reflecting a valid consumer concern about complexity and privacy.

comments.author: jmspring
comments.text:
I have a 2016 Tacoma I bought in 2015. It has ~114k miles, so ~11k miles/year. Gas is 16-18gal/mi. It's paid off. There is no math, outside of major repairs (it's maintained regularly) where any Hybrid or EV makes sense for the next 10+ years. Maintenance ~ 250 a year; Tires ~12-1300 every 3 years (more due to age than wear). So - 11k/year w/ fuel at $5/gal and 16mi/gal - $3.4k in fuel, 600/year in maintenance and tires. So $4k/year in rough cost (excluding insurance). Still high, but I've lived in rural areas the last 10 years.

A new vehicle makes no sense. Unless I went a budget used Prius (with a good hybrid battery system). No plan to make changes.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a realistic cost analysis from a rural perspective, explaining why switching to EVs or hybrids may not be economically justified for some.

comments.author: gcanyon
comments.text:
EVs are going to be an extinction-level event for carmakers.

As the buggy-makers failed to transition to making cars, and thus ceased to be, so too will automakers fail to transition to EVs, and thus end their viability as vehicle manufacturers.
comments.analysis: The comment uses historical analogy to emphasize the existential threat EVs pose to traditional automakers, a widely accepted industry concern.

comments.author: getpokedagain
comments.text: The software designed car and continued price growth of automobiles is going to push them out of price range for consumers. Maybe Honda just wants to go out of a dying industry on good terms.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights affordability concerns and suggests Honda's retreat might be a strategic exit, a plausible interpretation given market trends.
